summary: An overview of Plato's ethics, tracing his development from the early aporetic dialogues featuring Socratic cross-examination to the constructive, systematic theories of his middle period. It highlights his eudaemonistic focus on virtue, the theory of recollection, the tripartite division of the soul, and the analogy between the just city and the just individual.

principles.statement: Happiness is the highest aim of moral conduct, and the virtues are the skills needed to attain it.
principles.rationale: Human actions serve an ultimate purpose, and well-being (eudaimonia) is the ultimate goal of a life worth living, achievable only through excellence of character and mind.
principles.source_quote: happiness or well-being ( eudaimonia ) is the highest aim of moral thought and conduct, and the virtues ( aretê : ‘excellence’) are the dispositions/skills needed to attain it.

principles.statement: Justice consists in doing your own work and not meddling in the affairs of others.
principles.rationale: Both society and the individual soul function best when each part performs the specific task it is naturally most fit for, creating harmony and order.
principles.source_quote: doing your own thing and not meddling with that of others

principles.statement: True knowledge requires tying down beliefs with an account of the reason why.
principles.rationale: True beliefs are fleeting and unreliable on their own; they only become stable knowledge when interconnected through a deep understanding of their underlying causes and relations.
principles.source_quote: After they are tied down, in the first place, they become knowledge, and then they remain in place.

principles.statement: Society will only be free from evil when philosophers become rulers.
principles.rationale: Only those who possess true wisdom and have undergone rigorous intellectual and moral training can properly guide the state toward the Good, rather than toward wealth or power.
principles.source_quote: Until philosophers rule as kings or those who are now called kings and leaders genuinely and adequately philosophize ... cities will have no rest from evils, nor will the human race.

frameworks.name: Socratic Cross-Examination (Elenchos)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When challenging unquestioned traditional values, exposing unfounded self-confidence, or seeking the true definition of a virtue.
steps: Ask an alleged expert to provide an account or definition of a specific virtue.
Test the definition to see if it is merely an enumeration of examples, too broad, too narrow, or logically inconsistent.
Expose the flaws in the interlocutor's argument to induce a state of perplexity (aporia).
Use this intermediary stage of perplexity to pave the way for discovering true general definitions.
frameworks.source_quote: When they are confronted with their inability to explain the nature of their cherished virtue or expertise, they end up admitting their ignorance

frameworks.name: The Division of Functions
frameworks.when_to_apply: When structuring a just society or analyzing the proper order of an individual's soul.
steps: Identify the distinct needs of the whole (e.g., economic provision, protection, leadership).
Determine the natural fitness and abilities of the available parts or individuals.
Assign each person or part to the single job they are naturally most fit for ('one person - one job').
Ensure no part meddles with the functions of the other parts.
frameworks.source_quote: It is best if everyone performs the task s/he is naturally most fit for.

mental_models.name: The City-Soul Analogy
mental_models.description: A macro/micro lens for understanding complex moral concepts. To understand a difficult concept like justice in the individual (the 'small text'), first examine it in the structure of the state (the 'larger text'), assuming an isomorphic structure between the two.
mental_models.example: Dividing the soul into rational, spirited, and appetitive parts to perfectly mirror the ruling, military, and producing classes of a city.

mental_models.name: Justice as Health / Injustice as Disease
mental_models.description: An analogy comparing the moral state of the soul to the physical state of the body. Justice is the natural harmony and proper functioning of the soul's parts, while injustice is an internal rebellion and disorder.
mental_models.example: Arguing that just as no one in their right mind would prefer to live with a ruined, diseased body, no one should prefer to live with a diseased, unjust soul, regardless of external rewards.

mental_models.name: The Statues of Daedalus
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the difference between true belief and knowledge. True beliefs are valuable but prone to 'running away' (being forgotten or abandoned under pressure) unless they are 'tied down' by a rational understanding of why they are true.

heuristics: Search for the one feature common to all cases when defining a concept.
The unexamined life is not worth living.

signature_quotes.text: the unexamined life is not worth living
signature_quotes.context: Socrates' justification for challenging his fellows on an individual basis.

signature_quotes.text: Until philosophers rule as kings or those who are now called kings and leaders genuinely and adequately philosophize ... cities will have no rest from evils, nor will the human race.
signature_quotes.context: The provocative thesis introducing the need for philosopher-kings in the Republic.

signature_quotes.text: doing your own thing and not meddling with that of others
signature_quotes.context: The ultimate definition of justice in the state, discovered by a process of elimination.

signature_quotes.text: As the whole of nature is akin, and the soul has learned everything, nothing prevents a man, after recalling one thing only – a process men call learning – discovering everything else for himself
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the theory of recollection (anamnesis) and the interconnectedness of knowledge.

anti_patterns.description: Defining a concept by merely enumerating examples of it.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It fails to capture the nature, essence, or the single invariant feature (eidos/idea) that is common to all cases of the virtue in question.

anti_patterns.description: Living in accordance with pre-established societal standards and conventions without reflection.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Conventional 'virtues' are often geared towards wealth, power, and self-indulgence, which undermines public morality and damages the individual's soul.

anti_patterns.description: Allowing the appetitive or spirited parts of the soul to rule over reason.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates a rebellion within the soul, resulting in the inappropriate rule of inferior parts, which is the very definition of injustice and psychological disease.
